---
title: "Week 6 -- Exploratory Data Analysis II: ggplot2 and Visual Thinking"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Build layered visualizations using ggplot2 grammar of graphics
- Choose appropriate geometry for your data type and research question
- Map variables to aesthetics (position, color, size, shape) effectively
- Create faceted plots to compare groups or individuals
- Add annotations, reference lines, and text to communicate clinical context
- Export publication-ready figures in appropriate format and resolution
- Apply visual design principles: data-ink ratio, color accessibility, typography

## Why Visualization Matters

Numbers describe. Pictures convince. A well-designed plot can:

- Reveal patterns invisible in tables (clusters, trends, outliers)
- Communicate findings to clinicians who will not read your statistics
- Support or refute assumptions required for statistical tests
- Identify data quality issues (impossible values, gaps, duplicates)

**The goal is not a pretty picture. The goal is a true picture that is easy to understand.**

## Core Concepts

### The Grammar of Graphics

ggplot2 is built on a grammar: every plot is a mapping from data to visual elements.

| Component | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Data** | The dataset | `data = patients` |
| **Aesthetics** | Visual properties mapped to variables | `aes(x = age, y = map, color = group)` |
| **Geometry** | The visual mark representing data | `geom_point(), geom_line(), geom_boxplot()` |
| **Scales** | How data values map to visual values | `scale_x_log10(), scale_color_viridis_d()` |
| **Facets** | Small multiples by group | `facet_wrap(~ patient_id)` |
| **Themes** | Non-data visual adjustments | `theme_minimal(), theme_bw()` |
| **Coordinates** | The coordinate system | `coord_flip(), coord_cartesian()` |

Every ggplot follows this template:

```r
ggplot(data = <DATA>) +
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>(mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>)) +
  <SCALE_FUNCTION> +
  <FACET_FUNCTION> +
  <THEME_FUNCTION>
```

### Aesthetics: Mapping vs. Setting

**Critical distinction:** Mapping inside `aes()` vs. setting outside `aes()`.

```r
# WRONG: color is a constant, not a variable
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map, color = "red")) + geom_line()

# RIGHT: color mapped to a variable inside aes()
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map, color = patient_id)) + geom_line()

# RIGHT: constant color set outside aes()
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = map)) + geom_line(color = "red")
```

| Aesthetic | Maps Variable To | Clinical Example |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| `x`, `y` | Position on axes | Time, MAP, dose |
| `color` | Color of points/lines | Treatment group, severity |
| `fill` | Interior color of shapes | Histogram bins, boxplot boxes |
| `size` | Diameter of points | Sample size, uncertainty |
| `shape` | Point symbol | Categorical group |
| `alpha` | Transparency | Overlap density |
| `linetype` | Line pattern | Predicted vs. observed |

### Geometry Selection Guide

| Question | Geometry | Clinical Example |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| How is X distributed? | `geom_histogram(), geom_density()` | Distribution of ICU LOS |
| How do X and Y relate? | `geom_point(), geom_smooth()` | Dose vs. response |
| How does Y change over time? | `geom_line()` | MAP during surgery |
| How does Y differ by group? | `geom_boxplot(), geom_violin()` | MAP by ASA class |
| What are the proportions? | `geom_bar(), geom_col()` | Complication rates |
| Where are the thresholds? | `geom_hline(), geom_vline()` | MAP = 65 mmHg line |

### Facets: When to Use Them

Facets create small multiples -- the same plot repeated for subsets of data.

```r
# Compare patients side by side
ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, ncol = 2)
```

| Facet Function | Use When | Example |
|---------------|----------|---------|
| `facet_wrap(~ var)` | One categorical variable, many levels | One panel per patient |
| `facet_grid(row ~ col)` | Two categorical variables | Rows = treatment, columns = timepoint |
| `facet_wrap(~ var, scales = "free_y")` | Different y-ranges per group | Vital signs with different baselines |

**Rule:** Use `scales = "fixed"` (default) when you want to compare magnitudes across panels. Use `scales = "free"` when the shape matters more than the absolute values.

## Exercise

### Part 1: Create Simulated Intraoperative MAP Data

Generate a dataset of MAP (mean arterial pressure) measurements over time for 10 patients undergoing surgery:

```r
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(42)

# 10 patients, each with baseline MAP and 120 minutes of readings
patient_ids <- 101:110
baseline_map <- round(rnorm(10, mean = 85, sd = 10))

# Create time series: every 2 minutes for 120 minutes
time_points <- seq(0, 120, by = 2)

# Expand to long format
map_data <- expand.grid(
  patient_id = patient_ids,
  time_min = time_points
) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  arrange(patient_id, time_min) %>%
  mutate(
    baseline = baseline_map[match(patient_id, patient_ids)],
    # Induction drop (time 0): MAP falls 20-40% from baseline
    induction_drop = if_else(
      time_min == 0,
      baseline * runif(1, -0.4, -0.2),
      0
    ),
    # Maintenance: baseline + small fluctuations (+/-10%)
    maintenance_noise = rnorm(n(), 0, 5),
    # Events: spikes during intubation (time 3), incision (time 10), extubation (time 110)
    event_spike = if_else(
      time_min %in% c(3, 10, 110),
      runif(n(), 15, 25),
      0
    ),
    # Combine
    map = baseline + induction_drop + maintenance_noise + event_spike
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  # Ensure MAP stays in realistic range (40-180)
  mutate(map = pmax(pmin(map, 180), 40)) %>%
  # Add event labels
  mutate(
    event = case_when(
      time_min == 0 ~ "Induction",
      time_min == 3 ~ "Intubation",
      time_min == 10 ~ "Incision",
      time_min == 110 ~ "Extubation",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

head(map_data)
```

### Part 2: Basic Line Plot

Create a line plot of MAP over time for all patients on one panel:

```r
ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map, group = patient_id)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Intraoperative MAP: 10 Cases",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Questions:**
- What patterns do you see?
- Can you identify the induction drop?
- Can you identify the event spikes?
- Is it easy to compare individual patients?

### Part 3: Facet by Patient

Create small multiples to compare patients side by side:

```r
ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "MAP Trajectories by Patient",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Questions:**
- How does this compare to the overlaid version?
- Which patient had the lowest baseline MAP?
- Which patient had the largest event spike at intubation?
- What does the y-axis scale tell you about comparing across patients?

### Part 4: Add Event Annotations

Enhance the faceted plot by marking events with vertical lines and labels:

```r
# Get unique events with their times
event_times <- map_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(event)) %>%
  distinct(time_min, event)

ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_vline(
    data = event_times,
    aes(xintercept = time_min),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = "gray50"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = event_times,
    aes(x = time_min, y = Inf, label = event),
    vjust = 1.5,
    angle = 90,
    size = 3,
    color = "gray30"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "MAP with Annotated Surgical Events",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Questions:**
- Do the dashed lines help or clutter?
- Are the event labels readable?
- Can you see the MAP response to each event?

### Part 5: Highlight Hypotension Episodes

Add a horizontal reference line at MAP = 65 mmHg (clinical threshold for hypotension) and shade regions where MAP < 65:

```r
# Identify hypotension periods per patient
hypotension_data <- map_data %>%
  group_by(patient_id) %>%
  mutate(
    is_hypotensive = map < 65,
    # Create runs of consecutive hypotensive periods
    hypotension_run = rle_id(is_hypotensive & !is.na(is_hypotensive))
  ) %>%
  filter(is_hypotensive) %>%
  group_by(patient_id, hypotension_run) %>%
  summarise(
    start_time = min(time_min),
    end_time = max(time_min),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  # Shade hypotension regions
  geom_rect(
    data = hypotension_data,
    aes(xmin = start_time, xmax = end_time, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
    fill = "red",
    alpha = 0.1
  ) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 65, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "MAP with Hypotension Episodes Highlighted",
    subtitle = "Red shading: MAP < 65 mmHg",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Questions:**
- Which patient had the longest hypotension episode?
- Which event triggered the most widespread hypotension?
- Does the shading help or distract?

### Part 6: Create a Summary Dashboard

Combine multiple views into a single multi-panel figure using `patchwork`:

```r
library(patchwork)

p1 <- ggplot(map_data, aes(x = map)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "MAP Distribution (All Patients)",
    x = "MAP (mmHg)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- map_data %>%
  group_by(patient_id) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_map = mean(map),
    min_map = min(map),
    max_map = max(map),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(patient_id, mean_map), y = mean_map)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = min_map, ymax = max_map), width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean MAP by Patient",
    x = "Patient ID",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

p3 <- ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = patient_id), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 65, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "All Patients Overlaid",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4 <- ggplot(map_data, aes(x = time_min, y = map)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Individual Trajectories",
    x = "Time from Induction (minutes)",
    y = "MAP (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

(p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Intraoperative MAP Analysis Dashboard",
    subtitle = "Distribution, summary, and time-series views"
  )
```

**Reflection:** Which panel tells you what? When would you use each?

### Part 7: Export Your Figure

Save your favorite plot to a file:

```r
ggsave(
  filename = "map_time_series.png",
  width = 10,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300,
  units = "in"
)
```

Use `pdf()` for vector graphics suitable for publication:

```r
ggsave("map_time_series.pdf", width = 8, height = 4)
```

## Common Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Prevent |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| "My lines are all jumbled" | Forgot `group = patient_id` in `aes()` | Always specify grouping for time series with multiple subjects |
| "Colors are ugly" | Default palette is not colorblind-safe | Use `scale_color_viridis_d()` or `scale_color_brewer()` |
| "Axis labels are cut off" | Margins too small | Use `ggsave(..., width, height)` with sufficient size; adjust `theme(plot.margin = ...)` |
| "Facets have different y-axis scales" | `scales = "free_y"` | Use `facet_wrap(..., scales = "fixed")` for comparison |
| "Legend is missing" | Aesthetic mapped inside `geom_*` instead of `ggplot()` | Put `aes(color = ...)` in `ggplot()`, not `geom_line(aes(...))` |
| "Plot is slow with 100,000 points" | Overplotting | Use `geom_bin2d()` for density, or sample for exploration |
| "I can not find the outlier" | Overplotting hides points | Use `alpha` transparency, or `geom_point(position = "jitter")` |
| "Date axis is unreadable" | Default formatting | Use `scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b %d")` |

## Visual Design Principles for Clinical Plots

### 1. Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte)

Maximize the proportion of ink that represents data. Minimize:

- Unnecessary grid lines (use `theme_minimal()` or `theme_classic()`)
- Background shading (avoid `theme_bw()` for publication)
- Decorative borders
- 3D effects (never use `3d` in clinical plots)

**Good:** Clean axes, direct labeling, no chartjunk.

### 2. Color Choice

- **Sequential data** (intensity): Use single-hue gradients (blues, grays)
- **Diverging data** (above/below threshold): Use two-hue gradients (red-blue centered at white)
- **Categorical data** (groups): Use perceptually distinct, colorblind-safe palettes
  - `scale_color_viridis_d()` -- default for discrete
  - `scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")` -- ColorBrewer qualitative
- **Never use rainbow** -- misleading order, not colorblind-safe

**Clinical accessibility:** ~8% of males have red-green colorblindness. Test with `viridis` or `colorblindr::cvd_grid()`.

### 3. Typography

- Use sans-serif fonts for presentations (Arial, Helvetica)
- Use serif fonts for print publications (Times, Palatino)
- Axis text: 9-11 pt for publication, larger for slides
- Title: 12-14 pt for publication, 24-36 pt for slides
- Avoid all-caps axis labels (harder to read)

### 4. Aspect Ratio

Choose width:height ratio to reveal patterns:

- Time series: wide (16:9 or 4:3) -- shows trends clearly
- Scatter plots: square (1:1) if variables on same scale
- Bar charts: moderate width -- bars should not be too thin or too thick

**Rule:** The plot should fit the medium without squinting.

### 5. Direct Labeling

Avoid legends when possible. Label lines directly:

```r
ggplot(data, aes(x = time, y = value, color = group)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = group),
    hjust = 1,
    vjust = 1,
    check_overlap = TRUE
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

Reduces eye movement, improves comprehension.

## Verification

Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

- [ ] Build a ggplot from `ggplot(data, aes(...)) + geom_*()`
- [ ] Choose appropriate geometry for the data type and question
- [ ] Map variables to aesthetics inside `aes()`
- [ ] Set constant aesthetics outside `aes()`
- [ ] Customize labels with `labs()`
- [ ] Apply a clean theme (`theme_minimal()`)
- [ ] Create faceted plots with `facet_wrap()` or `facet_grid()`
- [ ] Add reference lines (`geom_hline(), geom_vline()`)
- [ ] Annotate plots with text (`geom_text()`)
- [ ] Save plots with `ggsave()` in appropriate format and resolution
- [ ] Push your Quarto document with embedded plots to GitHub

## Resources

- **ggplot2 Reference:** https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ -- Complete function list
- **R for Data Science (2e):** https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-visualize.html -- Visualization chapter
- **ggplot2 Cheat Sheet:** https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/main/data-visualization-2.1.pdf -- Quick reference
- **ColorBrewer Palettes:** https://colorbrewer2.org/ -- Choose perceptually uniform color schemes
- **Graphical Principles:** Edward Tufte, *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information* -- Classic on data-ink ratio
- **Clinical Visualization:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0735675721000275 -- Best practices for medical plots

---

[<- Back to Basic Tier](../index.qmd)
